titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Stephen Reinhardt Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Eric Sonnenberg

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday January 24, 2024 at 08:26

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Series II is open for research. Series I is closed until September 27, 2023.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Mark Reinhardt, Dana Reinhardt, and Justin Reinhardt, 2018.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The papers are arranged in two series: I. Case Files. II. Writings and Speeches.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Stephen Roy Reinhardt was a federal judge, lawyer, and writer. Reinhardt was born Stephen Shapiro on March 27, 1931, in New York City. He changed his surname after his mother remarried in his childhood. He received an undergraduate degree from Pomona College in 1951, and an LL. B from Yale Law School in 1954. After graduating, Reinhardt served in the Air Force general counsel's office before going into private practice in 1957, focusing on entertainment and labor law. Reinhardt was politically active, and served on the Democratic National Committee from California. He served on the California Advisory Committee of the United States Commission on Civil Rights from 1962 to 1974, and on the Los Angeles Police Commission from 1975 to 1980.

Reinhardt was appointed to the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit by President Jimmy Carter in November 1979, and confirmed by the Senate in September 1980. During his long career on the bench he was an outspoken figure in the federal courts, known as a "liberal lion" whose decisions were often at odds with a more conservative Supreme Court. Reinhardt's opinions included widely-debated decisions on capital punishment (Thompson v. Calderon), assisted suicide (Compassion in Dying v. Washington), same-sex marriage (Perry v. Brown), separation of church and state (Newdow v. United States Congress), and many other topics. During his judicial career he spoke and wrote regularly on legal and judicial topics, both in legal journals and in the popular press.
Reinhardt married three times, and had three children, Dana, Justin, and Mark. He served on the Ninth Circuit until his death on March 29, 2018.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Stephen Reinhardt Papers (MS 2096). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: All materials were reboxed and refoldered, and materials were arranged as described in the description for each series. Duplicate materials were removed. Notepad pages were detached from notepad backings and foldered individually during processing. Some fragile newsprint materials were replaced by photocopies for preservation purposes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Stephen Reinhardt papers document Reinhardt's service as a judge on the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit, as well as his work as a legal writer and speaker. Materials primarily document his judicial career, and include only a small amount of material from his career prior to his appointment to the Ninth Circuit. Personal material is not included.

The papers consist of case files from Reinhardt's judicial career, as well as files related to articles and speeches by or about Reinhardt. The case files include opinion drafts, memoranda, and other internal court documents, and date from throughout Reinhardt's tenure on the Ninth Circuit. The papers include only those cases for which Reinhardt wrote an opinion. The papers also include files on articles and speeches written by Reinhardt. Reinhardt's writings discuss a range of legal topics including the federal judiciary and Supreme Court, civil rights and civil liberties, and liberal jurisprudence and constitutional interpretation.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright and other associated proprietary rights for unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by Stephen Reinhardt was transferred to Yale University in 2018. These materials may be used for non-commercial purposes without seeking permission from Yale University as the copyright holder. For other uses of these materials, please contact beinecke.library@yale.edu. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

subject.part: United States. Court of Appeals (9th Circuit)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains draft and published opinions, memorandum dispositions, correspondence, memoranda, notes, and other material related to cases for which Stephen Reinhardt wrote an opinion during his career as a judge on the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 9th Circuit.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials are arranged in alphabetical order by case title.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Series I is closed until September 27, 2023 per donor agreement.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series consists of typescript and manuscript drafts, offprints, magazines, and clippings of speeches and articles written by or about Stephen Reinhardt. Materials also include event programs, photographs, and related correspondence.

did.unittitle: Clippings about Reinhardt and his career
"An Analysis of the McCone Commission Report"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Report produced by the California Advisory Committee to the United States Commission on Civil Rights, of which Reinhardt was a vice-chairman

did.unittitle: "Limiting Access to the Federal Courts: Round Up The Usual Victims," Whittier Law Review
"The American Committee of Jurists: Monitoring the Case of Moses Mayekiso"
"Arbitration and the Courts: Is the Honeymoon Over?"
"The Conflict Between Text and Precedent In Constitutional Adjudication," Cornell Law Review
"Jewishness and Judging: A Judge's Thoughts on Two Jewish Justices," Cardozo Law Review
"Must We Rush the Executioner?" Los Angeles Times

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Speech to University of Chicago alumni group in Los Angeles

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Speech for National Jewish Law Students Association, March 4, 1994

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Speech at Federalist Society National Symposium on the Future of Civil Rights Laws at Stanford University

did.unittitle: "Jews, Jewish Justices, and the Supreme Court"
"Civil Rights and the New Federal Judiciary: The Retreat from Fairness"
"Liberalism and the American Jewish Legal Tradition"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Speech at Jewish Perspectives on the Law conference, Harvard Law School
